coredata.dc:title: Enhancing electricity generation of microbial fuel cell for wastewater treatment using nitrogen-doped carbon dots-supported carbon paper anode
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Yan-Fang
Zhang, Feng
Huang, Bao-Cheng
Yu, Han-Qing
coredata.dc:description: Microbial fuel cell is a promising technology to harvest energy from wastewater, and effective anode catalysts are essential for its applications. In this study, zero-dimension nitrogen-doped carbon dots, as biocompatible efficient modifiers, were synthesized and decorated onto the carbon paper surface to prepare microbial fuel cell anode. The as-fabricated nitrogen-doped carbon dots decorated carbon paper anode was able to enhance electricity generation and efficient wastewater treatment. It was found that the as-fabricated electrode had a high hydrophilicity, which was favorable for the microbial immobilization. Compared with the carbon paper anode, the biofilm on the as-prepared anode was almost twice of thickness. After introducing nitrogen-doped carbon dots, the extracellular electron transfer process from the microorganisms to the anode was enhanced. The microbial fuel cell with the as-developed anode generated a remarkable power output of 0.32 mW, 1.1 times higher than that of the raw carbon paper anode with same measurement area. Moreover, 16 sRNA pyrosequencing analysis shows that the quantity of Pseudomonas on the electrode increased by ∼40%, which also promoted the electron transfer rate by excreting mediators like phenazines or phenazine derivatives. This work developed a new approach to design microbial fuel cell anode material and fabricate a high-efficiency and environmentally friendly anode for electricity generation from wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy harvest
Wastewater treatment
Microbial fuel cell
Anode material
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Extracellular electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode
Characteristics of biofilm on MFC anode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFC0406303 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21590812 , 51538011 , and 51821006 ) and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University of the Ministry of Education of China for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) XPS spectra for the carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. (a) Element compositions of the carbon paper electrode (green column) and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (orange column). (b–c) Deconvolution of (b) N 1s and (c) C 1s for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. (d–e) Fluorescence microscopy characterization for (d) the carbon paper electrode and (e) the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode under UV mode. (f) Water contact angle of the carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. Average values were calculated from the contact angle measurements on at least 10 random locations for each electrode.

Electrode performance in chemical system. (a) Cyclic voltammetry (1 mV s-1) response of different electrodes (carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode) in PBS solution with pH of 7.0. The potential means the relative potential to Ag/AgCl. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for carbon paper electrode (green dot) and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (orange dot) at open circuit potential. Conditions: the electrode was placed into 10 mM K₃Fe(CN)₆/K4 [Fe(CN)₆ (1:1) and 0.1 M KCl solution, and given an amplitude of 5 mV with frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. (c) Plot of Ep vs lnv.

(a) Performance of current output over time of MFC with carbon paper electrode (green blank dot) and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (orange solid dot). Electrochemical workstation was used to collect the current data with an initial potential of −0.2 V. (b) Potential and power output of MFC with carbon paper electrode (green blank dot) and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (orange solid dot).

Performance of biofilm. (a–b) CLSM characterization of biofilm inoculated on (a) carbon paper electrode and (b) N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. (c) Quantity of protein of biofilm by BCA Protein Assays. (d) Amperometric response over time for different biofilm electrodes with same electrode area at 0 V. (e) Cyclic voltammetry (1 mV s-1) response of different biofilm electrodes (carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode) in PBS solution with pH of 7.0. The potential means the relative potential to Ag/AgCl. (f) UV–vis spectra of culture from MFCs with carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode after the power output reach steady-state.

(a) Species abundance of gene level in biofilms cultured on carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. (b) Excitation emission matrix of culture inoculated by N-doped CDs@carbon paper electorde.

head.ce:title: Enhancing electricity generation of microbial fuel cell for wastewater treatment using nitrogen-doped carbon dots-supported carbon paper anode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science & Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion
University of Science & Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science & Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial fuel cell is a promising technology to harvest energy from wastewater, and effective anode catalysts are essential for its applications. In this study, zero-dimension nitrogen-doped carbon dots, as biocompatible efficient modifiers, were synthesized and decorated onto the carbon paper surface to prepare microbial fuel cell anode. The as-fabricated nitrogen-doped carbon dots decorated carbon paper anode was able to enhance electricity generation and efficient wastewater treatment. It was found that the as-fabricated electrode had a high hydrophilicity, which was favorable for the microbial immobilization. Compared with the carbon paper anode, the biofilm on the as-prepared anode was almost twice of thickness. After introducing nitrogen-doped carbon dots, the extracellular electron transfer process from the microorganisms to the anode was enhanced. The microbial fuel cell with the as-developed anode generated a remarkable power output of 0.32 mW, 1.1 times higher than that of the raw carbon paper anode with same measurement area. Moreover, 16 sRNA pyrosequencing analysis shows that the quantity of Pseudomonas on the electrode increased by ∼40%, which also promoted the electron transfer rate by excreting mediators like phenazines or phenazine derivatives. This work developed a new approach to design microbial fuel cell anode material and fabricate a high-efficiency and environmentally friendly anode for electricity generation from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Activated sludge is the most widely used technology to treat municipal wastewater (Meng et al., 2016). However, at the present stage activated sludge process is energy intensive and requires high investment and operating costs. In activated sludge treatment process, a considerable amount of greenhouse gases and other volatile substances are released into air, and simultaneously excess sludge is produced and needs further disposal, resulting in the huge consumption of energy and secondary environmental pollution (Dignac et al., 2000; Świerczek et al., 2018). In fact, wastewater treatment has a potential to become a sustainable process if suitable technologies are adopted. Therefore, extracting organic chemicals or energy from wastewater will substantially reduce the wastewater treatment costs and conserve energy (Li et al., 2017; Sözen et al., 2019). In this context, microbial fuel cell (MFC) may provide an effective, sustainable and environmentally friendly route in wastewater treatment process (Gude, 2016). MFC is able to harvest electricity power through converting chemical energy in wastewater through the metabolism of microorganisms (Logan and Regan, 2006). Unfortunately, now the power output of MFC is still substantially lower than the theoretical value, limiting their practical applications in real-world scenario. Among all factors that restrict the power output of MFC, anode material is the critical component that impacts electron transfer and governs the whole MFC performance (Sonawane et al., 2017; Xiong et al., 2018). Therefore, selecting and fabricating efficient anode materials with excellent biocompatibility, fabulous stability in microbial inoculum mixtures, and remarkable low electron transfer resistance have received increasing interests to address the challenge.

Currently, carbon-based materials, such as ligno-cellulosic materials (Marzorati et al., 2018), carbon felt (Zhong et al., 2018), carbon paper (He et al., 2012), carbon brush (Xu et al., 2018), and graphite (Zhang et al., 2016a), have been commonly used as anode materials because they can increase the MFC electricity generation. However, they usually have a relatively low level of microbial inoculum and electron transfer efficiency. One of the promising solutions to resolve the problem is to modify the carbon-based anodes to enhance the microbial immobilization, reduce the electron transfer barrier, and thereby promote the MFC electricity generation.

Carbon quantum dots or carbon nanoparticle dots with a diameter less than 10 nm have witnessed rapid development over the past few years and provided a bright promise in various applications, e.g., cellular imaging (Zhu et al., 2013), biosensing (Medintz et al., 2005), and drug transferring (Wang et al., 2013), due to their brilliant biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. In addition, the broad optical adsorption and high adsorption coefficients of luminescent carbon dots that endow them a good candidate to fabricate photovoltaic and photocatalytic devices (Safardoust-Hojaghan and Salavati-Niasari, 2017; Yang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). Meanwhile, doping heteroatoms like nitrogen could effectively tune the performance of carbon dots because of its easily bonding with carbon atom (Gong et al., 2009). Thus, the nitrogen-doped carbon-dots (N-doped CDs) have attracted interests in catalytic applications because of their distinct physicochemical properties, i.e., large quantity of defects at the surface and edges, which will form more catalytic sites to develop effective catalytic activities (Li et al., 2012). For example, N-doped graphene quantum dots nanocomposite material was used for photocatalytic degradation of pollutants (Safardoust-Hojaghan and Salavati-Niasari, 2017). The photodegradation efficiency increased by 45% after the introduction of quantum dots under UV light. Such an improved photodegradation performance was attributed to the transfer of excited electrons from quantum dots to titanium dioxide, inducing generation of active oxygen and hydrogen radical to oxide pollutants. However, the pristine carbon dots possessed a poor electricity conductivity due to the poor crystallinity compared with inorganic quantum dots, which to some extent restricted the electron transfer efficiency (Li et al., 2015). Thus, it was proposed to support the CDs on an appropriate conductive substrate to improve catalyst attachment and electron transfer (Zhang et al., 2016b).

Inspired by the above results, biocompatible N-doped CDs, were proposed as “designed additives” to decorate on the carbon paper surface, and their electricity-generating capacity as an MFC anode was evaluated. First, the surface physiochemical properties were examined. Then, the electrocatalytic performance as an MFC anode and the related charge transfer resistance was explored. Furthermore, the microbial biofilm was characterized and species diversity was identified to elucidate the impact of N-doped CDs on the electrode performance. In addition, the mechanisms for the enhanced electricity generation by decorating with N-doped CDs were demonstrated through physicochemical and electrochemical analyses.

Ethylene glycol (≥99.0%), ethylenediamine (≥99.0%), N, N-dimethylacetamide (≥99.0%) and hexadecyltrimethylammonium chloride were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China. Carbon paper was purchased from Toray Co., Japan. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) tablet and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) (≥98.0%), obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., USA, were used to stain. Deionized (DI) water from a Milli-Q ultrapure water purification system (Millipore Co., USA) was used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped CDs solution was prepared following the procedures reported previously (Guan et al., 2017). Briefly, ethylene glycol (25 mL) was heated for 5 h at 200 °C. Then the obtained yellow and homogeneous solution was left to cool until it reached room temperature, ethylenediamine (1.35 mL) was dropped. The reaction was allowed for 5 h at 180 °C after stirring for 30 min. The N-doped CDs were collected subsequently. Electrochemical deposition approach was adopted to fabricate the N-doped CDs deposited carbon paper (N-doped CDs@carbon paper) electrode using a slightly modified method reported in a previous study (Liu et al., 2014). N-doped CDs solution used for electrochemical deposition was prepared by sonicated dissolving 168.5 mg of hexadecyltrimethylammonium chloride into 10 mL N-doped CDs and 25 mL N, N-dimethylacetamide solution. Carbon paper (2.5 × 2.5 cm2) was dipped into N-doped CDs solution, followed by electrochemical deposition at −30 V for 10 min. The carbon paper electrode was taken away from the N-doped CDs solution, followed by rinsing with a Mili-Q water thoroughly. Finally, N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode was obtained.

The primary surface chemical elements of electrodes and oxidation states of the elements were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, VG Instrument Co., USA) equipped with Al Kα as the exciting source. The wettability of electrodes was measured by a water contact angle system (JC 2000G, Zhongchen Co., China) to evaluate the surface wettability. Ten time measurements on different locations were conducted for each electrode and the data were calculated to obtain an average water contact angle value. The fluorescence properties and morphology of electrodes were imaged by fluorescence microscope (BX51, Olympus Co., Japan) with 120 W mercury lamp for radiation. The biofilm attached on the electrodes was imaged by a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (LSM 5 Pa, Zeiss, Jena Co., Germany). DAPI (a nucleic acid sensor) was allowed to stain samples for 30 min then the excess stain was carefully removed through rinsing twice with phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.0). Anti-fluorescent quencher was dropped onto samples before observation. The microbial biomass was quantified by protein concentration. BCA Assay Kit (Sangon Biotech Co., China) was used for quantification. The UV–Vis absorption spectra and excitation emission (Ex/Em) matrix were collected by a UV–Vis spectroscopy (Model 2450, Shimadzu Co., Japan) and fluorescence spectroscopy (Aqualog, Horiba Co., Japan), respectively.

H-shape dual-chambered MFC were constructed with 100 mL of working volume of each chamber. The double-chamber was separated with Nafion 117 cation exchange membrane (DuPont Inc., USA). The anode chamber was filled with active sludge (Jingkaiqu Co., China). The N-doped CDs@carbon paper was used as the modified electrode and the carbon paper as a control electrode. The surface area of the anode was 2.5 × 2.5 cm2. Recipes of the artificial wastewater of the MFC anode chamber was reported in literature (Zhu et al., 2015). It contained sodium acetate (2 g L-1), KH2PO4 (0.53 g L-1), K2HPO4 (1.07 g L-1), CaCl2 (0.02 g L-1), MgSO4 (0.015 g L-1), NaHCO3 (0.1 g L-1), NH4Cl (0.15 g L-1), NaCl (0.5 g L-1), and 1 mL of trace element. The trace element was reported by Angenent and Sung (2001). Its concentrated solution was composited by FeSO4·7H2O (70 g L-1), ZnCl2 (1.25 g L-1), MnCl2·4H2O (12.5 g L-1), (NH4)6Mo9O24·4H2O (1.25 g L-1), CoCl2·6H2O (3.75 g L-1), NiCl2·6H2O (2.5 g L-1), CuCl2·2H2O (0.75 g L-1), and H3BO3 (1.25 g L-1). Carbon felt was used as the cathode electrode with an apparent area of 2.5 × 2.5 cm2. The cathode chamber was full of potassium ferricyanide, Na2HPO4, and NaH2PO4.

The power output of the MFC was automatically collected every 30 s on a multi-potentiostat electrochemical workstation (CHI1030A, CHI Instruments Co., China) with an initial potential of −0.2 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760D, CHI Instruments Co., China). Pt wire was chosen as the counter electrode. Ag/Ag+ electrode was selected as the reference electrode. The LSV was measured from an open circuit voltage to 0 V versus Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 1 mV s-1. Power density (P) was calculated by P = I x V (I refers to current, V indicates potential). The scan rate of CV was 10 mV s-1 from −0.8 V to 0 V. EIS was carried out under the open circuit voltage. The electrode was placed into 10 mM K₃Fe(CN)₆/K4 [Fe(CN)₆ (1:1) and 0.1 M KCl solution. The frequency range was between 100 kHz and 0.1 Hz with amplitude set as 5 mV. Then, the anode resistance could be calculated based on the Nyquist plots of impedance spectra.

The carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode with attached biofilm were cut into small pieces after the voltage reached steady-state. The small pieces were placed into centrifuge tube. DNA was obtained from the pieces by Ezup bacterial DNA Purification Kit (Sangon Biotechnology Co., China) following the protocol provided by the manufacturer. After DNA extraction, carbon paper was removed from the tube by centrifugation at 6000 rpm and then the extraction solution was stored in −20 °C for 16S rRNA gene amplicons. First, diluted genomic DNA was used as a template, 16S rRNA gene amplicon libraries were then fabricated based on High-Fidelity PCR Master Mix (Phusion, New England Biolabs Inc., USA). In this process, two PCR primers (515F/806R) were selected against the V4 region of the 16S rRNA to identify the bacterial diversity. Followed by mixing the PCR products thoroughly and identifying with 2% agarose gel electrophoresis. Finally, the sequencing libraries were constructed and quantified with the DNA PCR-Free Sample Preparation Kit (TruSeq, Illumina Inc., USA) and Q-PCR, respectively. All the high throughput sequencing operations were performed on Illumina MiSeq platform in Novogene Co., China. The 16S rRNA gene amplicons, with 97% similarity, were identified into OTUs.

Physicochemical properties of electrode surface not only affect the microbial attachment, but also impact electron transfer, thus and accordingly the power output of MFC (Govind, 2015). Thus, the physicochemical properties including chemical oxidation states, the existence of conduct matter of electrode surface, and hydrophilicity of electrode surface were characterized.

To evaluate the chemical constitution of the carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs decorated carbon paper (N-doped CDs@carbon paper) electrode, their XPS spectra were analyzed (Fig. 1 a–c). The carbon content of the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode decreased by ∼4%. Meanwhile, for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode, the elemental content of oxygen and nitrogen increased by ∼15% and ∼56%, respectively, when compared with carbon paper electrode (Fig. 1a). A higher content of oxygen usually enable stronger adsorption of O2, which is beneficial to microbial metabolism on anode (Deng et al., 2016). The increased amount of nitrogen was due to the introduction of N-doped CDs, which had a high level of nitrogen. Specifically, the deconvolution of N 1s energy spectrum of displayed three peaks (Fig. 1b), which are ascribed into pyridinic N (398.2 eV), pyrrolic N (400.1 eV), and N–O species (402.5 eV) (Sun et al., 2014). The pyridinic N and pyrrolic N help to efficiently adsorb O2 and promote oxidation of organic matter on the electrode (Deng et al., 2016; Ding et al., 2013). In addition, these N bonds are associated with the change of electrode conductivity by reducing energy barrier (Ding et al., 2013; Souto et al., 1998). The C 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (Fig. 1c), which are assigned to sp2-hybridized carbon in graphite-like carbon (284.8 eV), C–N bonds (286 eV), and sp3-hybridized carbon in β–C3N4–like bonds (288 eV) (Jiang et al., 2002; Wei et al., 2009). Especially, graphite-like carbon is related to honeycomb lattice arrangement of carbon atoms in N-doped CDs (Wei et al., 2009). These results indicate the success incorporation of N-doped CDs into the carbon paper electrode, which introduced more active catalytical sites and decreased the resistance of charge transfer.

To further demonstrate the successful incorporation of N-doped CDs onto carbon paper, fluorescence microscopy under 120 W mercury lamp irradiation was used. As shown in Fig. 1d and e, the carbon paper electrode displayed black, i.e., no fluorescent property (Fig. 1d). While it exhibited blue fluorescence under the mercury lamp irradiation for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (Fig. 1e). This result indicates the existence of N-doped CDs on the carbon paper surface.

Hydrophilic electrode has been proven to enhance microbial affinity and electricity generation (Du et al., 2017; Ponzio et al., 2016). Thus, water contact angle measurements were conducted to examine the hydrophilicity of electrode surface. As shown in Fig. 1f, the carbon paper electrode had an average water contact angle of ∼112°, indicating its hydrophobic surface. However, after modifying the electrode with N-doped CDs the water contact angle decreased to ∼10°, suggesting its highly hydrophilicity surface. The decrease in water contact angle was attributed to the introduction of hydrophilic groups from the N-doped CDs (Fan et al., 2014; Gong et al., 2009).

To confirm the decreased electron transfer resistance was beneficial to enhancing electricity generation, electrochemical properties (i.e., CV and EIS) of the carbon paper electrode and N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode were explored in PBS solution buffered at pH 7.0. A higher current response was observed for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (0.088 A) than that of carbon paper electrode (0.012 A) at the same measurement area (Fig. 2 a). Remarkably, the oxidative and reductive curves of N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode exhibited wide separation, indicating the elevated electronic capacitance after introducing N-doped CDs. A similar result was reported previously (You et al., 2017). Additionally, the charge transfer resistance (Rct), meaning the diameter of a semi-circle (Jo et al., 2011), was revealed by Nyquist plots. As shown in Fig. 2b, compared with ∼116 Ω for the carbon paper electrode, ∼10 Ω of charge transfer resistance was observed for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. The smaller charge transfer resistance of the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode, suggesting rapid charge transfer from electrolyte to electrode, might be resulted from the enhanced conductive contact area (Feng et al., 2016). Furthermore, the electron transfer resistance was estimated by the linear response of Ep (peak-to-peak separation, corresponding to the electron transfer and diffusion) to lnv (Feng et al., 2006). As shown in Fig. 2c, the carbon paper electrode had a charge transfer coefficient k of 0.051. The N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode exhibited a lower charge transfer coefficient k (0.035), indicating a faster charge transfer for the modified electrode. Thus, the excellent electrochemical performance of the as-prepared electrode was associated with the doping of N-doped CDs, which reduced the energy barrier of catalytic activities and enhanced the electron transfer rate (Ding et al., 2013).

It is assumed that anode materials with enhanced microbial immobilization and reduced the electron transfer barrier would improve the MFC performance. Therefore, the performance of the MFC with the N-doped CDs@carbon paper anode was examined in an H-shape two-chamber reactor with a total volume of 100 mL and recorded by a three-electrode system on a multi-potentiostat electrochemical workstation. The anode chamber was cultured with activated sludge and potassium ferricyanide served at the cathode chamber. A shorter start-up time and higher current output were apparently observed for the MFC with the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode (Fig. 3 a) within half-month operation. This result is in agreement with the faster electron transfer efficiency of the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. An obvious overshoot phenomenon, evidenced by the bending inwards of curves and signals of a system limitation (Winfield et al., 2011), was observed (Fig. 3a). This observation could be attributed to the high scan-rate, 1 mV s-1 in this work, and induced the microbial inability to adjust to each new and more demanding resistance value (Nam et al., 2010; Winfield et al., 2011). The current for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode was around 2.3–3.3 mA (Fig. 3b), which was ∼2-fold higher compared with carbon paper electrode (0.7–1.2 mA). Moreover, the MFC with the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode also produced a higher power of 0.32 mW, a value about 77% higher than that of the bare carbon paper electrode (0.15 mW). These results prove that the electrode with N-doped CDs modification generated higher electricity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of biofilm on MFC anode
ce:section.ce:para: The quantity of microbial immobilization on the anode affects the power output of MFC. To characterize the microbes attached onto the electrode. CLSM was used to observe the biofilm when the electrodes reached a stable voltage in the MFC. Compared with the carbon paper electrode with thin biofilm thickness of ∼40 μm (Fig. 4 a), more compact and thicker biofilm was found on the electrode modified with the N-doped CDs (∼80 μm) (Fig. 4b). Apart from the CLSM characterization, the total biomass on the electrodes was quantified by BCA Assay. The total biomass increased by ∼68% for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode in comparison to the carbon paper electrode (Fig. 4c). These results prove that more microorganisms attached onto the N-doped CDs electrode surface, resulting in a higher electricity generation.

Microorganisms form biofilm and attach on electrode surface, affording notable conversion capacity and chances for extracellular electron transfer (Read et al., 2010). Therefore, to further elucidate the role of N-doped CDs in extracellular electron transfer process, a three-electrode cell was used to measure the chronoamperometric at 0 V verse Ag/AgCl electrode. The oxidation current was found to increase steeply for both electrodes, indicating the microorganisms attached and proliferated on the electrode surface. Especially, the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode exhibited a higher current (∼9 mA) than the carbon paper electrode (∼7.5 mA) (Fig. 4d). This result is consistent with the more immobilized microorganisms on the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode. Then, the oxidation current dropped sharply, indicating the organic substances became exhausted. The electrodes with biofilm after inoculation were immediately transferred into PBS solution buffered at pH 7.0. Cyclic volumetric measurements were performed thereafter. The N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode displayed a reduction peak at −0.33 V (Fig. 4e), attributed to the outer membrane cytochrome (OmcA) species, and an oxidation peak at 0.33 V. This result indicates that the electrochemical process on the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode surface was reversible. The carbon paper electrode had a cathodic peak at −0.17 V. However, there was no anodic peak for the carbon paper electrode. The asymmetric peaks suggest that the electro-oxidation was limited. This observation was assumed to the unique properties of OmcA species at the electronic active sites after incorporating N-doped CDs.

The existence of OmcA species was further confirmed using UV–vis spectroscopy by analyzing the culture supernatant from the anode chamber. There was a peak at ∼402 nm for the culture inoculated with the carbon paper (Fig. 4f), which came from the Soret absorbance band of oxidized form of outer membrane cytochrome (OmcA) (Yu et al., 2018). There were two peaks located at ∼518 and ∼522 nm for the culture inoculated by the N-doped CDs@carbon paper anode, which were ascribed to the Q-band of reduced form of OmcA (Shi et al., 2006). This result suggests that the N-doped CDs on the as-developed anode reduced microbial OmcA species.

To reveal microbial community diversity and structure in the formed biofilm on the electrodes, 16S rRNA gene sequences were analyzed and the microbial compositions of the two electrodes were compared at the gene level. Pseudomonas and Geobacter were major microbes that played significant role in MFC power output (Fig. 5 a). Specifically, the relative abundance of Geobacter was high for both electrodes (73.4% for the carbon paper electrode and 73.8% for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper electrode), consistent with the previously reported result that Geobacter plays a primary role in MFC inoculated from activated sludge (Qiao et al., 2017). While the relative abundance of Pseudomonas increased by ∼40% for the N-doped CDs@carbon paper anode in comparison to the carbon paper anode. Pseudomonas, a typical exoelectrogen strain, is able to easily form biofilms (Qiao et al., 2017), confirming a thicker biofilm as visualized in the CLSM imaging. Moreover, this strain promoted the extracellular electron transfer by excreting phenazines or phenazine derivatives (Kumar et al., 2015; Rabaey et al., 2005). As observed in the chronoamperometric result, rapid extracellular electron transfer was obtained for the modified electrode.

Possible phenazine derivatives were identified through extracting the anode supernatant from the culture and analyzing the fluorescence excitation-emission matrix (Fig. 5b). There were two fluorescent peaks for the culture, and the maximum excitation (Ex) peaks were situated at 280 nm and 420 nm, respectively. While the maximum emission (Em) peaks were located at 350 nm and 480 nm, separately. The peak of 280/350 nm (Ex/Em) was ascribed into protein-like substances (Coble, 1996). The peak of 420/480 nm (Ex/Em) was possibly caused by the excreted phenazines or phenazine derivatives (Banerjee, 2016).

In summary, the carbon paper-supported biocompatible zero-dimension N-doped CDs was fabricated as a novel MFC anode for high electricity generation and efficient wastewater treatment. The as-developed electrode exhibited a highly hydrophilic surface (∼10°) in comparison with the raw carbon paper electrode (∼112°) and benefited for the microbial immobilization. In addition, this electrode displayed a smaller charge transfer barrier (∼10 Ω), suggesting the enhanced interaction between microbes and the anodes. The MFC with the N-doped CDs@carbon paper anode displayed a higher power density (∼0.32 mW) than that with the carbon paper electrode (∼0.15 mW). The outperforming performance was attributed to the introduction of N-doped CDs. The microbial community analysis shows that the relative abundance of Pseudomonas was significantly enhanced for the as-prepared electrode (40% higher than that of the electrode without modification). The approach of using N-doped CDs as the “designed additives” could also be a versatile method to fabricate other electrode materials for MFC to enhance energy harvest from wastewater treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC0406303), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21590812, 51538011, and 51821006) and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University of the Ministry of Education of China for supporting this work.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Angenent and Sung, 2001

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of anaerobic migrating blanket reactor (AMBR), a novel anaerobic treatment system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Angenent, L.T., Sung, S., 2001. Development of anaerobic migrating blanket reactor (AMBR), a novel anaerobic treatment system. Water Res., 35, 1739-1747.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Banerjee, 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phenazines as chemosensors of solution analytes and as sensitizers in organic photovoltaics
